@graph.name: Mercedes-Benz models fitted with the M136 engine

mainEntity.name: How much does an M136 engine replacement cost?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M136 typically costs £2,000–£3,500 supply only, with fitting adding £500–£800 depending on labour rates [ESTIMATED]. Used examples can be found from £500, making it relatively affordable for a classic engine.

mainEntity.name: Is the M136 a reliable engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M136 rates 4.5★ out of 5 — widely regarded as exceptionally robust for its era. Its triple-bearing forged steel crankshaft and chain-driven camshaft contribute to impressive durability in both civilian and utility applications. Regular maintenance is the key to longevity.

mainEntity.name: What is the most common M136 failure?

acceptedAnswer.text: Carburettor and ignition system wear are the most common issues on surviving examples — the Solex updraught carburettor and distributor components degrade with age. Oil leaks from gaskets and seals are also typical on high-mileage or long-stored engines.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes models use the M136?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M136 powers the W136 170 V (1935–1950), W191 170 S variants (1950–1955), and the early W120 Ponton 180 (1953–1957) — spanning Mercedes' pre-war and early post-war passenger car range.

mainEntity.name: How long does the M136 last?

acceptedAnswer.text: With proper maintenance, 200,000+ miles is achievable — many well-maintained W136 and W191 examples remain on the road today. The simple side-valve design means fewer components to fail, though age-related wear on carburettor, ignition, and gaskets is inevitable.

hero.h1: Mercedes-Benz M136 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost
hero.subHeadline: The Mercedes M136 is a robust side-valve straight-four — a reliable pre-war workhorse that powered the iconic 170 V. Backed by real UK classic specialist data [ESTIMATED].

verdictRating.confidence: 82% confidence — based on classic Mercedes specialist consensus, owner-forum reporting, and period documentation.

rows.score: 16 (Simple side-valve design; most repairs straightforward for classic specialists — parts availability is the main constraint)
12 (Available via classic Mercedes specialists, but some parts are NLA or require specialist sourcing)
18 (Reconditioned cost as % of lowest host value — well under 30% for restored examples → 18)

verdictRating.oneLineVerdict: "The M136 is a famously tough, simple pre-war four — side-valve design is dated but durable — buy on originality and documented history."
verdictRating.bestFor: Classic Mercedes enthusiasts wanting an original, reliable pre-war or early post-war driving experience.
verdictRating.avoidIf: Buyers seeking modern performance, fuel economy, or easy parts availability from main dealers.

costGuide.labourEstimate: Fitting an M136 typically takes 8–10 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent classic specialist) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE specialist) [ESTIMATED].
